the Court that he was ready and willing to perform his part of the contract. It is hence, in this case,
whether time is the essence of sale of immovable properties or should the plaintiff ought to have
filed the suit immediately on the expiry of the 11 months period which is fixed in Ext.A.1, matter
significantly.
12.This Court finds that there is considerable merit in the finding of the first Appellate Court that
the plaintiff has not proved that he was ready and willing to http://www.judis.nic.in perform his
part of the contract.
13.Looking from another angle, irrespective of the fact that the fourth defendant had not pleaded
that he was a bonafide purchaser for value without notice of Ext.A.1 in terms of Section 19(b) of the
Specific Relief Act, inasmuch as the relief sought is a equitable relief, the plaintiff still may require to
prove that the fourth defendant had notice of Ext.A.1 before he purchased the property under
Ext.B.1 sale deed. After all, even if the fourth defendant had pleaded so, yet, he cannot prove the
negative, for the burden will still be on the plaintiff. Since the plaintiff seeks a relief in equity, he has
to walk the extra distance to satisfy the Court's conscience that he is entitled to obtain a decree for
specific performance.
14.This Court does not find any perversity in the findings of the first Appellate Court, though the
reasons for arriving at the same finding may have a mild variance. All the substantial questions of
law fails. This Court does not find any merit in the appeal.
15.In the result, the second appeal is dismissed and the judgement and decree, dated 12.07.2000,
passed in A.S.No.34 of 1999 on the file of the III Additional District Judge, Krishnagiri reversing the
judgment and decree, dated 08.02.1999, passed in O.S.No.138 of 1994 on the file of Subordinate
Judge, Dharmapuri is http://www.judis.nic.in upheld. No costs.
12.03.2020 Index:Yes/No Internet:Yes/No tsg To:
1.The III Additional District Court, Krishnagiri.
2.The Subordinate Court, Dharmapuri.
3.The Section Officer VR Section, High Court, Madras.
http://www.judis.nic.in N.Seshasayee,J., tsg 12.03.2020 http://www.judis.nic.in

title: Smt Iravva K Purantar vs S Venkatesh on 21 November, 2023
text:
Smt Iravva K Purantar vs S Venkatesh on 21 November, 2023
Author: S.R.Krishna Kumar
Bench: S.R.Krishna Kumar
                                            -1-
                                                         NC: 2023:KHC:41743
                                                        RFA No. 172 of 2018
                      IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU
                        DATED THIS THE 21ST DAY OF NOVEMBER, 2023
                                         BEFORE
                        THE HON'BLE MR JUSTICE S.R.KRISHNA KUMAR
                         REGULAR FIRST APPEAL NO. 172 OF 2018 (SP)
            BETWEEN:
            SMT. IRAVVA K PURANTAR,
            W/O SRI. T. NAGAPPA,
            AGED ABOUT 70 YEARS,
            RESIDING AT 134, NAGANANDA NILAYA,
            16TH CROSS, 7TH MAIN,
            4TH PHASE, J. P. NAGAR,
            BENGALURU - 560 078.
                                                                 ...APPELLANT
            (BY SRI. SHARATH N, ADVOCATE FOR
                SRI. SHIVA KUMAR, ADVOCATE AND
                SRI. VEDAVASYA RAO R.S, ADVOCATE)
            AND:
            1.     S. VENKATESH,
                   S/O SRI. SURYANARAYANA SHASTRY,
                   AGED ABOUT 54 YEARS,
Digitally          RESIDING AT JARAGANAHALLI,
signed by          J.P.NAGAR, BENGALURU - 560 078.
VANDANA S
Location:
            2.     THE SECRETARY,
HIGH
COURT OF           KENDRA UPADYAYARA SANGHA (REGD.),
KARNATAKA          NO.24, SUBBARAMA CHETTY ROAD,
                   BASAVANAGUDI, BENGALURU - 560 004.
                                                             ...RESPONDENTS
            (BY SRI. JANARDHANA G.N, ADVOCATE FOR R1;
                SRI. K.P. BHUVAN, ADVOCATE FOR R2)
                    THIS RFA IS FILED UNDER SEC.96 READ WITH ORDER 41 RULE 1
            OF THE CPC., AGAINST THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE DATED 15.12.2015
            PASSED IN OS NO.3810/2010 ON THE FILE OF THE XVIII ADDL.CITY CIVIL
                                  -2-
                                                 NC: 2023:KHC:41743
                                               RFA No. 172 of 2018
JUDGE AT BENGALURU CITY [CCH NO.10] DECREEING THE SUIT FOR
SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE.
      THIS APPEAL, COMING ON FOR ORDERS, THIS DAY, THE COURT
DELIVERED THE FOLLOWING:
                               JUDGMENT
This appeal is directed against the impugned judgment and decree dated 15.12.2015 passed in
O.S.No.3810/2010 by the XVIII Additional City Civil Judge, Bengaluru City, whereby the suit filed
by respondent No.1/plaintiff against respondent No.2/defendant for specific performance of
lease-cum-sale agreement dated 10.10.1986 in relation to the suit schedule immovable property was
decreed by the trial Court in favour of respondent No.1/plaintiff against the respondent
No.2/defendant.
2. Heard the arguments on both sides. The material on record discloses that respondent
No.1/plaintiff instituted the aforesaid suit for specific performance and other reliefs against the
respondent No.2/defendant. In the said suit, respondent No.1/plaintiff specifically contended that
respondent No.2/Sangha had executed lease-cum-sale agreement dated 10.10.1986 and issued a
possession certificate dated 18.12.1986 and had put the plaintiff in possession and enjoyment of the
suit schedule property.
NC: 2023:KHC:41743 It was contended that despite execution of the aforesaid document,
respondent No.2-Sangha was attempting to alienate the suit schedule property in favour of third
parties and as such, respondent No.1/plaintiff had instituted the said suit for specific performance
and other reliefs.
3. The respondent No.2/defendant filed his written statement inter alia disputing and denying the
various claims and contentions urged by the plaintiff. The defendant specifically contended that
plaintiff was an utter stranger and not a member of the defendant/Sangha. It was also contended
that pursuant to formation of a layout in Sy.Nos.45 and 47 of Jakkasandra Village, Begur Hobli,
Madivala I Stage, Bengaluru South Taluk, respondent No.2/Sangha had allotted site No.543 in
favour of the appellant herein vide registered lease-cum-sale agreement dated 17.09.1987 and ever
since appellant was in possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule property. Under these
circumstances, respondent No.2/Sangha sought for dismissal of the suit.
4. Based on the aforesaid pleadings, the trial Court framed the following issues.
NC: 2023:KHC:41743 "1) Whether the plaintiff proves that defendant feet as per sale consideration
receipt dated 02.10.1985 amounting to Rs.24, 026/- and also got executed lease cum sale agreement
dated 10.10.1986 and has agreed to executed registered sale deed within 10 years from the said date?
2) Whether the defendant proves that plaintiff is not the member of defendant's sangha and there is
no valid sale agreement and plaintiff is a stranger to the sangha?
3) Whether the defendant proves that sites were allotted to its member on lottery basis and site
No.543 was allotted to Smt.I.K.Puranther under registered lease cum sale agreement dated
17.09.1987?
4) Whether the defendant proves that plaintiff's cause of action is imaginary?
5) Whether the defendant proves that court fee paid is insufficient?
6) Whether the defendant proves that plaintiff has created forged document and has filed this suit?
7) Whether the plaintiff is entitled for the relief of specific performance of contract?
8)    What decree or order?"
                                                NC: 2023:KHC:41743
5. A perusal of the material on record including the impugned judgment and decree will indicate
that the plaintiff examined his GPA holder as PW.1 and marked Exs.P1 and P4 on his side. However,
the defendant/Sangha neither cross examined PW.1 nor adduced any oral or documentary evidence
in support of his defence. Under these circumstances, having regard to the uncontroverted and
unchallenged evidence of the plaintiff, the trial Court proceeded to decree the suit in his favour, by
passing the impugned judgment and decree, which is assailed in the present appeal.
6. A perusal of the memorandum of the appeal and the application filed on behalf of the appellant
will indicate that appellant has alleged that defendant-Sangha had executed a lease-cum-sale
agreement dated 17.09.1987 in his favour as contended in the written statement. In this context, it is
relevant to state that appellant has filed an application I.A.No.5/2018 for additional documents
under Order 41 Rule 27 producing the following documents:
"(1) Copy of the registered Lease-Cum-Sale Agreement dated 17.09.1987.
NC: 2023:KHC:41743 (2) Copy of the Possession Certificate dated 17.03.1988.
(3) Copy of the Sale Deed dated 19.03.2012 (4) Copy of the Khatha Certificate and Khata Extract.
      (5)    Copy    of      the    summons      and    plaint    in
      (6)    Copy of the plaint in O.S.No.3810/2010.
      (7)    Copy of the written statement of 2nd respondent
             in O.S.No.3810/2010. "
7. A perusal of the aforesaid documents comprising of the registered lease-cum-sale agreement
dated 17.09.1987, possession certificate dated 17.03.1988, sale deed dated 19.03.2012, katha
certificate, katha extract and other documents will indicate that the said documents are relevant and
necessary for adjudication of the present appeal particularly, in the light of the specific contention
urged on behalf of the defendant/Sanga in the suit that it had alienated the suit schedule property in
favour of the appellant herein as could be seen from the written statement filed on behalf of the
defendant/Sangha. Under these circumstances, I am of the considered opinion, that I.A.No.5/2018
filed for producing additional documents, deserves to be allowed and documents produced along
with the same, deserves to be received on record.
NC: 2023:KHC:41743
8. As stated supra, the appellant herein who claimed through and under the defendant/Sangha was
not arrayed as a party to the suit. However, in the light of the specific contention of the
defendant/Sangha that it had alienated the suit schedule property in favour of the appellant herein
and on the ground that her alleged right, title, interest and possession over the suit schedule
property would be affected by the impugned judgment and decree, therefore, the appellant has
preferred the present appeal along with I.A.No.2/2018 for permission to file the appeal. While
dealing with the applications, I.A.No.5/2018 for additional documents, I have already come to the
conclusion is that the said documents are relevant and necessary for the purpose of adjudication of
the issues in controversy between the parties, particularly, in the light of the specific contention of
the appellant and the defendant/Sanga that the suit schedule property was alienated in favour of the
appellant prior to institution of the suit. Under these circumstances, I am of the view that an
opportunity be provided in favour of the appellant to prosecute the present appeal and accordingly
I.A.No.2/2018 filed by the appellant is hereby allowed.
NC: 2023:KHC:41743
9. I.A.No.1/2018 is filed by the appellant seeking condonation of the delay of 677 days in filing the
appeal. In this regard, it is to be stated that pursuant to allowing I.A.No.5/2018 and I.A.No.2/2018
and providing an opportunity in favour of the appellant to prosecute the present appeal, I am of the
considered opinion that delay on the part of the appellant who was not a party to the suit was due to
bonafide reasons and unavoidable circumstances, and sufficient cause, since she had no knowledge
about the pendency of the suit particularly when the lease-cum- sale agreement dated 10.10.1986
sought to be enforced by the plaintiff in the suit was an unregistered agreement, whereas lease-
cum-sale agreement executed by the defendant/Sangha in favour of the appellant on 17.09.1987 was
a registered document. Under these circumstances, being satisfied that the delay in preferring the
present appeal, has been satisfactorily explained. I.A.No.1/2018 is hereby allowed and the delay in
preferring the appeal is hereby condoned.
10. Insofar as, I.A.No.3/2018 is filed by the appellant seeking impleadment as an additional
defendant to the suit is concerned, as stated supra, despite the appellant having obtained NC:
2023:KHC:41743 registered lease-cum-sale agreement on 17.09.1987 prior to the institution of the
suit in the year 2010, the appellant was not made party to the suit. A perusal of the said documents,
in favour of the appellant will indicate that she has putforth claim in respect of the very same suit
schedule property, which was subject matter of the suit and which culminated in the impugned
judgment and decree. While it is true that in a suit for specific performance, a non-party to the
contract would ordinarily not be construed or treated as a proper and necessary party, in the light of
the specific assertion on the part of the Sangha in the written statement, that the suit was liable to be
dismissed in view of execution of registered lease-cum- sale agreement dated 17.09.1987 in favour of
the appellant, prior to the institution of the suit and since defendant/Sangha did not exercise due
diligence and did not cross-examine PW.1 or adduced any oral or documentary evidence, on his
behalf, it would be necessary for the appellant to take suitable steps to safe guard her alleged right,
title, interest and possession of the suit schedule property by getting herself impleaded as additional
defendant in the suit. Under these circumstances, I am of the view that in the peculiar/special facts
and circumstances of the instant case, the appellant is both proper and necessary party to the suit
and is
- 10 -
NC: 2023:KHC:41743 entitled to be impleaded as an additional defendant No.2 in the suit.
Accordingly, I.A.No.3/2018 is allowed and appellant is permitted to be impleaded as additional
defendant No.2 in the suit.
11. The next question that arise for consideration is the procedure to be followed for the purpose of
disposal of the appeal as contemplated under Order 41 Rule 28 of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908.
While dealing with the applications, I.A.No.1/2018, 2/2018, 3/2018 and 5/2018, I have already
come to the conclusion that the appellant is both proper and necessary party to the suit and
documents produced along with I.A.No.5/2018 are relevant and necessary for the purpose of
adjudication of the issues in controversy between the parties. As stated earlier, the
defendant/Sangha neither cross examined PW.1 nor adduced any oral or documentary evidence on
his behalf. Further upon impleadment, an opportunity would have to be provided in favour of the
appellant to file her written statement as defendant No.2 and contest the suit on merits. Under these
circumstances, I am of the view that the impugned judgment and decree passed by the trial Court
deserves to set aside and the matter be remitted back to the
- 11 -
NC: 2023:KHC:41743 trial Court for reconsideration afresh in accordance with law. In the result, I
pass the following:
ORDER I. Appeal is hereby allowed.
II. Impugned judgment and decree dated 15.12.2015 is hereby set aside. III.
I.A.No.3/2018 filed by the appellant is allowed and appellant is directed to be
impleaded as additional defendant No.2 to the suit along with defendant
No.1/Sangha. IV. I.A.No.5/2018 filed for producing additional documents is allowed
and the documents produced by the appellant are received on record.
V. Registry is directed to transmit I.A.No.5/2018 along with documents to the trial
Court for reconsideration afresh in accordance with law. VI. The entire matter is also
remitted back to the trial Court for reconsideration afresh in accordance with law.
VII. Liberty is reserved in favor of the appellant/defendant No.2 to file her written
statement and contest the suit on merits. VIII. Liberty is also reserved in favour of all
parties to adduce evidence and also cross examine the other parties and their
witnesses.
- 12 -
NC: 2023:KHC:41743 IX. All rival contentions and all aspects of the matter are kept
open and no opinion is expressed on the same.
X. Appellant and respondent Nos.1 and 2 undertake to appear before the trial Court
without further notice on 18.12.2023.
Sd/-
JUDGE rv CT: BHK

title: Kum.H.Supriya vs S Krishnappa on 9 February, 2026
text:
Kum.H.Supriya vs S Krishnappa on 9 February, 2026
Author: S.R.Krishna Kumar
Bench: S.R.Krishna Kumar
                                                -1-

                                                        WP No. 37035 of 2025
                    HC-KAR
                        IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU
                             DATED THIS THE 9TH DAY OF FEBRUARY, 2026
                                             BEFORE

                        NO.242, "MATHRUSHREE NILAYA",

                   2.   SMT. SHOBHA K. N.
                        W/O LATE SRI MANJUNATHA H S

                   3.   SRI. H. M. HARSHA
                        S/O LATE SRI MANJUNATHA H S
                               -2-
                                            NC: 2026:KHC:7501
                                        WP No. 37035 of 2025
HC-KAR
    AGE ABOUT 32 YEARS
    BOTH ARE PRESENTLY
    R/AT C/O SRI. H. S. NAGARAJU
    NO.82, HINDUMARANAHALLI,
    DABBEGHATTA HOBLI,
    DABBEGHATTA POST,
    TURUVEKERE TALUK,
    TUMKUR DISTRICT - 572 227
                                         ...RESPONDENTS
(BY SRI. MANJEGOWDA D S., ADVOCATE FOR R1)
     THIS WRIT PETITION IS FILED UNDER ARTICLE 227 OF THE
CONSTITUTION OF INDIA RAYING TO a) BY QUASHING ORDER
DATED 17/11/2025 PASSED ON 1.A.NO.VIII IN O.S.NO.07/2018,
PENDING ON THE FILE OF SENIOR CIVIL JUDGE AND JMFC,
HOLENARASIPURA,      PRODUCED      AT   ANNEXURE-H;     b)
CONSEQUENTLY, THE PETITIONER PRAYS THAT THIS HON'BLE
COURT BE PLEASED TO ALLOW THE APPLICATION I.A.NO.VIII
FILED BY THE PETITIONER UNDER ORDER 6 RULE 17 READ WITH
SECTION 151 CPC IN O.S.07/2018, PENDING ON THE FILE OF
SENIOR CIVIL JUDGE AND JMFC, HOLENARASIPURA, PRODUCED
AT ANNEXURE-F; c) PASS SUCH OTHER ORDER/S AS THIS
HON'BLE COURT DEEMS FIT UNDER THE FACTS AND
CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE IN THE INTEREST OF JUSTICE
AND EQUITY.
    THIS PETITION, COMING ON FOR ORDERS THIS DAY,
ORDER WAS MADE THEREIN AS UNDER:
CORAM:   HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE S.R.KRISHNA KUMAR
                        ORAL ORDER
This petition by the defendant No.3 in O.S.No.7/2018 is directed against the impugned order dated
17.11.2025 passed by the Senior Civil Judge and JMFC, Holenarasipura, whereby the application
I.A.No.VIII filed by the petitioner- defendant No.3 NC: 2026:KHC:7501 HC-KAR seeking
permission to amend the written statement by incorporating additional paragraph No.17a after
paragraph 17 was rejected by the trial Court.
2. Heard learned counsel for the petitioner and learned counsel for the respondent No.1 and perused
the material on record. For the order proposed, service of notice to respondent Nos.2 and 3 are
dispensed with.
3. A perusal of the material on record will indicate that the respondent No.1-plaintiff instituted the
aforesaid suit against the petitioner - defendant No.1 and defendant Nos.2 and 3 for specific
performance of an alleged sale agreement dated 14.09.2018 relating to suit schedule immovable
property and for other reliefs. The defendants are contesting the said suit. The respondent No.1
having adduced evidence, and at the stage of cross-examination of PW1, the petitioner filed the
instant application seeking amendment of the written statement by incorporating certain
subsequent events in relation to suit in O.S.No.59/2021 pending on the file of the Senior Civil Judge,
Holenarasipura. The said application having been opposed by the plaintiff, the trial Court proceeded
to pass the NC: 2026:KHC:7501 HC-KAR impugned order rejecting the application, aggrieved by
which, the petitioner is before this Court by way of the present petition.
4. A perusal of the impugned order will indicate that the trial Court has rejected the application on
the ground that the petitioner had not exercised due diligence in not seeking amendment earlier
prior to filing the instant application. In this context, it is pertinent to note that the proposed
amendment relates to certain subsequent events that are alleged to have occurred after institution of
the suit including institution of O.S.No.59/2021, which obviously could not have been pleaded by
the petitioner- defendant No.3 at the time of filing the written statement in the suit of the year 2018.
5. Under these circumstances, in the light of the principles enunciated by the Hon'ble Apex Court in
the cases of LIC v. Sanjeev Builders (P) Ltd. - (2022) 16 SCC 1, Dinesh Goyal @ pappu v. Suman
Agarwal (bindal) & ors. - 2024 INSC 726 and a co-ordinate Bench of this Court at Dharwad Bench in
the case of Shri Mohammadrafi and Anr., Vs. Bandenawaz and Ors
- W.P.108512/2025 dated 16.12.2025, I am of the considered opinion that the impugned order
passed by the trial Court refusing NC: 2026:KHC:7501 HC-KAR amendment has occasioned failure
of justice warranting interference by this Court in the present petition. At any rate, since the plaintiff
would have an opportunity to file rejoinder/reply to the amended written statement, no prejudice
can be said to have been caused to the plaintiff if the proposed amendment was allowed and failure
on the part of the trial Court to appreciate this has resulted in erroneous conclusion.
6. In the result, I pass the following:
ORDER
i) The petition is hereby allowed.
ii) The impugned order dated 17.11.2025 on I.A.No.VIII in O.S.No.7/2018 on the file
of Senior Civil Judge and JMFC, Holenarasipura, is hereby set aside.
iii) I.A.No.VIII under Order VI Rule 17 of CPC is hereby allowed.
iv) Liberty is reserved in favour of the respondent No.1-
plaintiff to file rejoinder/reply to the amended written statement.
NC: 2026:KHC:7501 HC-KAR
v) The trial Court is directed to dispose of the suit within a period of one year from the date of
receipt of a copy of this order.
vi) All rival contentions on all aspects of the matter are kept open and no opinion is expressed on the
same.
Sd/-
(S.R.KRISHNA KUMAR) JUDGE MDS List No.: 1 Sl No.: 15
